note: Phase 3b historical-pattern library. Loaded once via /api/seminar/patterns/seed. Idempotent on lower(name). pattern_type vocabulary: leverage_stall | proxy_war | sanctions_breach | regime_change | great_power_shift | nuclear_brinkmanship | deterrence_signaling | alliance_realignment | blockade_siege | intervention | secession_partition | normalization_thaw | insurgency_counterinsurgency | economic_coercion | territorial_annexation | appeasement | decolonization.

patterns.name: Berlin Airlift
patterns.description: A great power tried to choke a contested city by cutting its land access, and was answered by a logistics campaign that called the bluff without firing a shot.
patterns.what_happened: After currency reform in the western zones, Stalin blockaded all road, rail and canal links into West Berlin to force the Allies out. Rather than fight through or withdraw, the US and UK flew in food, coal and supplies for nearly a year — at the peak landing a plane every few minutes.
patterns.outcome: Stalin lifted the blockade in May 1949 having gained nothing; the episode hardened the West, accelerated NATO's formation and the creation of West Germany, and set the template for resolute non-kinetic resistance.
patterns.lessons: A blockade is a test of resolve, not a fait accompli — a credible, sustained alternative can defeat coercion without escalation. Calling a bluff with logistics buys time and moral high ground.

patterns.name: Cuban Missile Crisis
patterns.description: Two nuclear powers walked to the edge over forward-deployed missiles, then found a face-saving off-ramp built on a secret reciprocal concession.
patterns.what_happened: The USSR secretly installed nuclear missiles in Cuba; the US discovered them via U-2 imagery and imposed a naval 'quarantine'. After thirteen days of brinkmanship, Khrushchev withdrew the missiles in exchange for a US no-invasion pledge and a secret removal of US Jupiter missiles from Turkey.
patterns.outcome: War was averted, a hotline was established, and both sides learned the costs of brinkmanship — yet the public 'US won' narrative obscured the quiet quid-pro-quo that actually closed the deal.
patterns.lessons: Deals at the brink often hinge on a concession one side can't admit publicly. Back-channels and asymmetric face-saving are what convert a standoff into a settlement; the visible 'win' is rarely the whole story.

patterns.name: U-2 Incident
patterns.description: A covert surveillance program collapsed in public when an asset was captured, handing the adversary an information and propaganda victory and torpedoing a summit.
patterns.what_happened: The USSR shot down a US U-2 spy plane and captured pilot Francis Gary Powers. Washington issued a cover story about a weather plane; Moscow then produced the live pilot and wreckage, exposing the lie and humiliating the administration days before the Paris summit.
patterns.outcome: The Paris summit collapsed, US–Soviet relations chilled, and the episode showed how a single exposed operation can detonate diplomacy and discredit official denials.
patterns.lessons: Cover stories built on the assumption an operation can't be proven will backfire catastrophically once evidence surfaces. Plausible deniability is only as strong as the adversary's inability to display the truth.

patterns.name: Suez Crisis
patterns.description: Declining empires launched a military intervention their own superpower patron refused to back, and were forced into a humiliating reversal that marked the end of an era.
patterns.what_happened: After Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal, Britain, France and Israel secretly colluded to seize it by force. The US, fearing Soviet inroads and global backlash, applied financial pressure (threatening the pound) and joined the USSR at the UN to demand withdrawal.
patterns.outcome: The invaders withdrew, British and French imperial pretensions were shattered, Nasser emerged a pan-Arab hero, and the episode confirmed that the US and USSR — not the old colonial powers — now set the limits of action.
patterns.lessons: Power is the ability to act without your patron's veto. Economic leverage (currency, financing) can halt a war faster than armies; a campaign your great-power backer opposes is doomed regardless of battlefield success.

patterns.name: Hungarian Uprising
patterns.description: A satellite state's bid for autonomy was crushed by its hegemon while a rhetorically supportive outside power declined to intervene in the hegemon's sphere.
patterns.what_happened: Hungarians rose against Soviet domination, formed a reform government and declared neutrality. The USSR invaded with tanks, killed thousands and reinstalled a loyalist regime. Despite 'rollback' rhetoric and Radio Free Europe broadcasts, the US offered no material help.
patterns.outcome: The revolt was crushed, 200,000 fled, and the limits of Western 'liberation' rhetoric inside the Soviet sphere were exposed — a tacit acknowledgment of spheres of influence.
patterns.lessons: Rhetorical encouragement without commitment can lure the vulnerable into doomed defiance. Hegemons enforce red lines brutally within their recognized sphere; outsiders rarely contest the core.

patterns.name: Korean War
patterns.description: A divided nation became the first hot battlefield of a global rivalry, where intervention, counter-intervention and a frozen stalemate produced an armistice but no peace.
patterns.what_happened: North Korea invaded the South; a US-led UN force repelled it and pushed north, prompting massive Chinese intervention when forces neared the Yalu. The front stabilized near the original line after enormous casualties.
patterns.outcome: An armistice in 1953 — never a peace treaty — froze the peninsula along the 38th parallel, militarized the Cold War globally, and created a 70-year unresolved standoff.
patterns.lessons: Crossing an adversary's threshold (approaching its border) can convert a limited war into a great-power clash. Frozen armistices can outlive everyone who signed them; 'temporary' divisions become permanent.

patterns.name: Vietnam Escalation
patterns.description: A superpower incrementally escalated into a counterinsurgency it could not win, driven by credibility logic and a contested pretext, until domestic will collapsed.
patterns.what_happened: Citing the Gulf of Tonkin incident, the US committed ground forces and a bombing campaign against a determined nationalist-communist insurgency. Escalation followed escalation under 'domino theory' and credibility arguments; the 1968 Tet Offensive shattered claims of progress.
patterns.outcome: Despite overwhelming firepower, the US could not break the insurgency, lost domestic support, and ultimately withdrew — a defining lesson in the limits of conventional power against a politically rooted enemy.
patterns.lessons: Credibility and sunk-cost logic drive escalation past the point of strategic sense. Firepower doesn't defeat a movement with popular roots and external sanctuary; pretexts unravel and erode trust.

patterns.name: Fall of the Berlin Wall
patterns.description: A hegemon's decision not to use force to hold its empire triggered a cascade that dissolved a bloc faster than anyone forecast.
patterns.what_happened: As reformist Moscow signaled it would not send tanks to prop up Eastern European regimes, protests swelled and a bungled press announcement opened the Berlin Wall overnight. The Eastern Bloc unraveled within months.
patterns.outcome: Germany reunified, the Warsaw Pact dissolved, and the Cold War order ended — demonstrating how quickly a system held together by coercion collapses once the coercion is withdrawn.
patterns.lessons: Empires sustained by the credible threat of force can implode the moment that threat is rescinded. Cascades are nonlinear: small triggers detonate when legitimacy is already hollow.

patterns.name: Iranian Revolution
patterns.description: A close client autocrat was toppled by a mass revolution that surprised his patron's intelligence, flipping a pillar of regional strategy into a long-term adversary.
patterns.what_happened: Mass protests across class and ideological lines overthrew the US-backed Shah; Ayatollah Khomeini established a theocratic republic. The US embassy hostage crisis followed, severing relations for decades.
patterns.outcome: A keystone US ally became a defining adversary; the regional balance, oil politics and US posture were reshaped for two generations, and the limits of propping up unpopular clients were exposed.
patterns.lessons: Investing in an unpopular strongman builds a brittle asset that can flip overnight. Intelligence services systematically miss revolutions because they track elites, not street legitimacy.

patterns.name: Iran–Iraq War
patterns.description: Outside powers quietly armed both sides of a grinding regional war to ensure neither won, normalizing horrors while pursuing a balance-of-power aim.
patterns.what_happened: Saddam Hussein invaded post-revolution Iran expecting a quick win; the war became an eight-year attritional bloodbath with trench warfare, missile strikes on cities and chemical weapons. The US and others tilted to keep Iran from prevailing while engaging in covert dealings with both.
patterns.outcome: A million-plus dead, no border changes, and a cynical demonstration that great powers may prefer a bleeding stalemate to either side's victory.
patterns.lessons: Balance-of-power logic can make sustaining a stalemate a deliberate goal. Arming both sides 'to keep it even' normalizes atrocity and entangles the broker in contradictions (e.g., arms-for-hostages).

patterns.name: Camp David Accords
patterns.description: A determined third-party mediator locked two recent enemies in seclusion and converted land-for-peace into a durable, if cold, bilateral normalization.
patterns.what_happened: President Carter brought Sadat and Begin to Camp David for thirteen days of intense, near-collapse negotiations. The framework traded the Sinai's return to Egypt for recognition and peace with Israel.
patterns.outcome: Egypt and Israel signed a peace that has held for decades; Egypt was suspended from the Arab League and Sadat was later assassinated, but the strategic realignment endured.
patterns.lessons: High-stakes mediation often requires a forcing environment, a committed broker spending political capital, and a concrete swap (territory for recognition). Durable peace can be cold and costly to its signers.

patterns.name: Soviet–Afghan War
patterns.description: A superpower intervened to save a failing client and was bled white by a foreign-armed insurgency, with blowback that outlasted the war.
patterns.what_happened: The USSR invaded to prop up a communist government and faced a mujahideen insurgency funded by the US (Operation Cyclone), Saudi money and Pakistani logistics. Stinger missiles and sanctuary across the border ground the Soviets down.
patterns.outcome: The USSR withdrew in 1989 having gained nothing, the war helped bankrupt the Soviet system, and the armed, radicalized networks that won later metastasized into transnational jihad.
patterns.lessons: Intervening to save a brittle client invites a costly insurgency. Arming proxies for a near-term win seeds long-term blowback; the sponsor rarely controls what it creates.

patterns.name: Taiwan Strait Crises
patterns.description: A rising power used shelling and missile tests around a contested island to signal resolve, and a distant power answered with carriers to reset deterrence.
patterns.what_happened: In the 1950s the PRC shelled offshore islands; in 1995–96 it fired missiles near Taiwan to intimidate ahead of elections. The US deployed carrier battle groups through the strait, signaling it would contest coercion.
patterns.outcome: Each crisis ended without war but hardened positions; the 1996 carrier transit became a lasting humiliation that drove China's long anti-access military buildup.
patterns.lessons: Coercive signaling invites counter-signaling; the visible humiliation of being out-deterred can fuel a decades-long capability response. Strait deterrence is a recurring, escalating cycle.

patterns.name: Nixon–Mao Opening to China
patterns.description: Two ideological enemies set aside hostility to align against a shared greater rival, executing a secret-diplomacy reversal that re-sorted the global chessboard.
patterns.what_happened: Amid the Sino-Soviet split, secret Kissinger trips paved Nixon's 1972 visit to Beijing. Washington and Beijing tacitly aligned against Moscow despite deep ideological enmity.
patterns.outcome: The triangular diplomacy pressured the USSR, reshaped the Cold War, and began China's path toward global integration — a textbook 'enemy of my enemy' realignment.
patterns.lessons: Shared threat trumps ideology in alliance formation. A rival's internal split is a strategic opening; bold realignments are built on secret diplomacy and balance-of-power calculation.

patterns.name: Tiananmen Square Crackdown
patterns.description: Unlike the contemporaneous Eastern Bloc, an authoritarian state chose decisive lethal force to crush reform pressure and preserve the regime — and it worked.
patterns.what_happened: Mass pro-democracy protests centered on Tiananmen Square were met with a military crackdown that killed hundreds to thousands. The Party reasserted control while pivoting to market reforms under continued one-party rule.
patterns.outcome: The regime survived, foreign condemnation faded as commerce resumed, and Beijing drew the durable lesson that controlled economic opening plus political repression can be sustainable.
patterns.lessons: Some regimes survive precisely because they're willing to use force the world expects them not to. Economic engagement frequently overrides moral outrage; sanctions over values often prove temporary.

patterns.name: Collapse of the Soviet Union
patterns.description: A superpower dissolved largely from within — economic exhaustion, loss of legitimacy and nationalist centrifugal forces — reordering the world into a unipolar moment.
patterns.what_happened: Economic stagnation, reform that loosened control (glasnost/perestroika), a failed hardliner coup, and republics declaring independence led the USSR to dissolve into fifteen states by December 1991.
patterns.outcome: The Cold War ended, the US became the sole superpower, vast nuclear arsenals had to be secured, and unresolved grievances over post-Soviet borders seeded future conflicts.
patterns.lessons: Great powers often fall from internal rot, not external defeat. Sudden collapses leave dangerous loose ends (weapons, borders, diasporas, revanchism) that shape the next era.

patterns.name: Gulf War — Liberation of Kuwait
patterns.description: A naked annexation triggered a broad, UN-sanctioned coalition that reversed it decisively — then deliberately stopped short of regime change.
patterns.what_happened: Iraq invaded and annexed Kuwait. A US-led coalition of 35 nations, with UN backing and Arab participation, expelled Iraqi forces in a 100-hour ground war after a sustained air campaign, but chose not to march on Baghdad.
patterns.outcome: Kuwait was restored, the norm against conquest reaffirmed, US primacy demonstrated — yet Saddam survived to fester, prefiguring the 2003 war and debates over 'finishing the job'.
patterns.lessons: Clear aggression enables broad coalitions and legitimacy. Limited aims preserve coalitions but leave the problem alive; the choice to stop short is itself consequential and contested.

patterns.name: Yugoslav Wars and Kosovo Intervention
patterns.description: A multiethnic state violently fragmented along national lines, producing ethnic cleansing, delayed outside intervention, and a precedent-setting bombing campaign without UN authorization.
patterns.what_happened: Yugoslavia's breakup spiraled into wars and atrocities (Srebrenica). After years of hesitation, NATO bombed Bosnian Serb forces (1995, leading to Dayton) and later Serbia over Kosovo (1999) without a Security Council mandate, citing humanitarian necessity.
patterns.outcome: New states emerged, Kosovo's status remained contested, and a precedent for humanitarian intervention bypassing the UN was set — later cited both for and against later interventions.
patterns.lessons: Multiethnic states under stress can shatter fast and bloodily. Intervention often comes too late; bypassing the UN for humanitarian aims creates precedents that cut both ways.

patterns.name: First Chechen War
patterns.description: A weakened great power tried to crush a separatist region by force, was fought to a humiliating stalemate, then returned years later with overwhelming brutality.
patterns.what_happened: Russia invaded to stop Chechen secession; its demoralized military suffered heavy losses and the devastating siege of Grozny failed to pacify the region, ending in a face-saving withdrawal. A second war (1999) reconquered it through far harsher methods.
patterns.outcome: The first war ended in de facto Chechen autonomy and Russian humiliation; the second 'solved' it through devastation and a loyal strongman, shaping Russia's later approach to force.
patterns.lessons: Counterinsurgency against a motivated population is brutal and rarely quick; initial humiliations breed harsher second attempts. Domestic-political weakness shows on the battlefield.

patterns.name: Iraq War / Regime Change in Baghdad
patterns.description: A preventive war justified by contested intelligence toppled a regime quickly but triggered an insurgency, sectarian war and a strategic windfall for a rival.
patterns.what_happened: Citing weapons of mass destruction (never found) and links to terrorism, a US-led coalition invaded and toppled Saddam in weeks. De-Baathification and a security vacuum spawned a long insurgency and sectarian civil war.
patterns.outcome: Trillions spent, hundreds of thousands dead, no WMD, and Iran emerged as the chief regional beneficiary — a defining lesson in the gap between toppling a regime and building order.
patterns.lessons: Removing a regime is easy; constructing legitimate order is not. Intelligence shaped to justify a decision corrodes credibility; toppling a rival's rival can empower a greater adversary.

patterns.name: Russo-Georgian War
patterns.description: A great power used a frozen conflict and a local provocation to invade a neighbor seeking Western alignment, then froze new 'independent' enclaves to block its NATO path.
patterns.what_happened: After months of friction and a Georgian move into South Ossetia, Russia launched a swift invasion, defeated Georgian forces and recognized South Ossetia and Abkhazia as independent. The West protested but did not intervene.
patterns.outcome: Russia entrenched control over the enclaves, signaled the costs of NATO aspiration on its borders, and tested Western resolve — a rehearsal for later moves in Ukraine.
patterns.lessons: Frozen conflicts are tools, not accidents; a manufactured or seized provocation can justify rapid faits accomplis. Blocking a neighbor's alignment by creating disputed territory is a repeatable playbook.

patterns.name: Annexation of Crimea
patterns.description: A great power seized strategic territory from a neighbor in revolutionary turmoil using deniable forces and a staged referendum, presenting a fait accompli the West met only with sanctions.
patterns.what_happened: After Ukraine's pro-Western revolution, unmarked Russian troops ('little green men') took control of Crimea; a hurried referendum 'ratified' annexation. Russia simultaneously fueled separatist war in the Donbas.
patterns.outcome: Crimea was annexed, Western sanctions imposed without reversing it, and an eight-year low-grade war in the east set the stage for the 2022 full-scale invasion.
patterns.lessons: Deniable 'hybrid' force plus a manufactured legitimacy ritual can create a hard-to-reverse fait accompli. Sanctions punish but rarely roll back a seizure; partial aggression often presages a larger one.

patterns.name: JCPOA — Iran Nuclear Deal
patterns.description: A multilateral bargain traded sanctions relief for verified nuclear limits, converting maximum economic pressure into a negotiated cap rather than a war or a bomb.
patterns.what_happened: Years of escalating sanctions and covert sabotage brought Iran to the table; the P5+1 and Iran agreed to constrain enrichment and accept intrusive inspections in exchange for phased relief.
patterns.outcome: Iran's program was verifiably constrained for years and a war was deferred, though critics attacked sunset clauses and the omission of missiles and proxies — leaving the deal politically fragile.
patterns.lessons: Sustained, multilateral economic pressure can yield a verifiable deal short of war. Agreements that omit adjacent issues (missiles, proxies) and rely on sunsets are durable only with continued political buy-in.

patterns.name: US Withdrawal from the JCPOA
patterns.description: A great power unilaterally exited a working multilateral deal and reimposed sanctions, betting maximum pressure would force a better bargain — and instead got escalation.
patterns.what_happened: Washington withdrew from the nuclear accord despite Iranian compliance, reimposing sweeping secondary sanctions. Iran responded by gradually exceeding enrichment limits and stepping up regional pressure.
patterns.outcome: No 'better deal' materialized; Iran advanced its program, regional tensions spiked (tanker attacks, Soleimani strike), and allied trust in US commitments was strained.
patterns.lessons: Tearing up a working agreement for hoped-for leverage can backfire, producing escalation rather than capitulation and eroding the credibility of your signature. Maximum pressure has diminishing returns against a resolved adversary.

patterns.name: Killing of Qassem Soleimani
patterns.description: A targeted strike on a top adversary commander aimed to restore deterrence, risking a wider war that both sides then carefully de-escalated.
patterns.what_happened: A US drone strike killed Iran's Quds Force commander in Baghdad after a series of proxy attacks. Iran retaliated with telegraphed ballistic missile strikes on US bases, calibrated to save face without forcing all-out war.
patterns.outcome: A full war was avoided, Iraq's parliament voted to expel US forces, and the strike reset — but did not end — the cycle of proxy escalation.
patterns.lessons: Decapitation strikes are high-variance deterrence: they can reset the board or ignite a war. Calibrated, face-saving retaliation is how adversaries avoid uncontrolled escalation after a shock.

patterns.name: Abraham Accords
patterns.description: Former adversaries normalized ties around a shared rival and tangible incentives, sidelining a long-standing precondition that had frozen regional diplomacy.
patterns.what_happened: Several Arab states normalized relations with Israel, brokered by the US with sweeteners (arms sales, recognition of Western Sahara claims). The Palestinian issue, long treated as a precondition, was effectively bypassed.
patterns.outcome: A new alignment around shared concern over Iran emerged, but unresolved Palestinian grievances remained a latent fault line that later violence exposed.
patterns.lessons: Shared threats plus concrete incentives can dissolve old taboos and preconditions. Bypassing a core grievance buys realignment but leaves a combustible unresolved issue beneath the surface.

patterns.name: Libya Intervention
patterns.description: A UN-authorized civilian-protection mission expanded into de facto regime change, removing a dictator but leaving a stateless vacuum and poisoning great-power trust.
patterns.what_happened: Citing imminent atrocities in Benghazi, the UNSC authorized a no-fly zone and 'all necessary measures.' NATO airpower went beyond protection to enable rebels to topple and kill Qaddafi.
patterns.outcome: Libya fragmented into warring militias, became a migration and arms conduit, and Russia and China — feeling deceived about the mandate's scope — blocked similar action on Syria.
patterns.lessons: Mission creep from protection to regime change destroys the legitimacy that enabled action. Toppling a strongman without a state-building plan invites chaos; perceived bait-and-switch hardens future vetoes.

patterns.name: Sahel Coups and the Wagner Pivot
patterns.description: A string of coups expelled a former colonial security partner and invited a great-power-backed mercenary force, swapping patrons amid persistent insurgency.
patterns.what_happened: Military juntas seized power across the Sahel, blamed French counterterrorism for failing against jihadist insurgencies, and turned to Russian Wagner mercenaries for regime security and a new patron relationship.
patterns.outcome: French forces withdrew, Russian influence and resource access grew, juntas consolidated, and insurgent violence continued or worsened — a regional realignment away from the West.
patterns.lessons: Security failure delegitimizes the incumbent patron and opens the door to rivals offering regime survival, no strings attached. Counterinsurgency that doesn't deliver security invites patron-swapping.

patterns.name: Venezuela — Guaidó Recognition Standoff
patterns.description: An external campaign of recognition and sanctions tried to dislodge an entrenched regime without force, and failed because the security services stayed loyal.
patterns.what_happened: The US and dozens of states recognized opposition leader Juan Guaidó as interim president and tightened oil sanctions, betting Maduro would fall. The military's loyalty held; Russia, China and Cuba propped the regime.
patterns.outcome: Maduro survived, the economy cratered and millions emigrated, and the limits of recognition-plus-sanctions against a coup-proofed regime were exposed.
patterns.lessons: Regimes fall when the men with guns defect — not when foreign capitals withdraw recognition. Sanctions can immiserate a population without toppling a coup-proofed elite backed by outside patrons.

patterns.name: Bay of Pigs Invasion
patterns.description: A covert proxy invasion built on wishful assumptions about a popular uprising collapsed on the beach, humiliating its sponsor and hardening the target.
patterns.what_happened: A CIA-trained Cuban exile brigade landed at the Bay of Pigs expecting to spark a revolt against Castro. Air support was withheld to preserve deniability; the brigade was quickly defeated and captured.
patterns.outcome: A fiasco that humiliated Washington, pushed Cuba closer to Moscow, and set up the missile crisis — a lesson in deniability undermining the operation it was meant to protect.
patterns.lessons: Plans premised on a hoped-for popular uprising usually fail. Withholding decisive support to keep deniability dooms the mission; covert regime change without local legitimacy backfires.

patterns.name: Chilean Coup of 1973
patterns.description: Covert economic and political pressure helped destabilize an elected government a great power disliked, culminating in a military coup and a long dictatorship.
patterns.what_happened: After Allende's socialist election, the US worked to 'make the economy scream' through covert funding, credit denial and support to opposition. The military under Pinochet seized power; Allende died in the palace.
patterns.outcome: A 17-year dictatorship with mass repression followed; the episode became a defining case of Cold War intervention in the Western Hemisphere and of indirect destabilization.
patterns.lessons: Economic strangulation and covert support can topple governments without overt invasion — but at lasting moral and reputational cost. Destabilization is deniable yet rarely stays hidden.

patterns.name: Rwandan Genocide and the Failure to Intervene
patterns.description: A foreseeable mass atrocity unfolded while outside powers — wary after a recent debacle — defined it away and declined to act until it was over.
patterns.what_happened: In about 100 days, extremists slaughtered roughly 800,000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu. The UN force was withdrawn rather than reinforced; powers avoided the word 'genocide' to dodge obligations, partly shadowed by the Somalia 'Black Hawk Down' trauma.
patterns.outcome: The genocide ran its course, became a lasting moral stain, and drove the later development of the 'Responsibility to Protect' doctrine.
patterns.lessons: Recent failures make powers gun-shy at the worst moment; bureaucratic language ('acts of genocide') can be used to avoid duty. Inaction is itself a fateful choice with its own costs.

patterns.name: Brexit
patterns.description: A major power voluntarily exited a deep economic-political bloc on a narrow referendum, trading integration for sovereignty amid years of costly disentanglement.
patterns.what_happened: A 52–48 referendum committed the UK to leaving the EU. Years of fraught negotiation over trade, borders (notably Northern Ireland) and rights followed before a managed exit.
patterns.outcome: The UK regained formal sovereignty but faced trade frictions, internal strains (Scotland, Northern Ireland) and reduced clout — a case study in the costs of disintegration.
patterns.lessons: Disentangling from deep integration is far costlier and slower than joining. Narrow mandates for irreversible change unleash prolonged uncertainty; sovereignty and market access trade off.

patterns.name: Eurozone Debt Crisis
patterns.description: A monetary union without fiscal union forced austerity on weaker members in exchange for bailouts, exposing creditor–debtor power asymmetries inside an alliance.
patterns.what_happened: Greece and other periphery states faced unsustainable debt; creditors (Germany, ECB, IMF) extended bailouts conditioned on severe austerity. A 2015 standoff nearly pushed Greece out of the euro.
patterns.outcome: The union held, but at the cost of deep recession, lasting resentment and a demonstration that shared currency without shared fiscal capacity concentrates power with creditors.
patterns.lessons: In an asymmetric economic union, creditors set terms and debtors absorb pain; conditionality is coercion by another name. Half-built institutions buckle under stress at the periphery.

patterns.name: North Korean Nuclear Breakout
patterns.description: A weak, isolated state pursued nuclear weapons as ultimate regime insurance, outlasting sanctions, talks and threats to become a de facto nuclear power.
patterns.what_happened: Pyongyang withdrew from the NPT, conducted repeated nuclear and missile tests, and cycled through negotiations, agreements and collapses (Six-Party Talks, summits) while steadily advancing its arsenal.
patterns.outcome: Sanctions and diplomacy failed to denuclearize the North; it achieved a credible deterrent, illustrating that determined proliferators can win if regime survival is the stake.
patterns.lessons: For a vulnerable regime, the bomb is the ultimate insurance — coercion rarely outweighs that calculus. Drawn-out talks can buy the proliferator time; observed cases (Libya, Iraq) teach adversaries not to disarm.

patterns.name: South China Sea Militarization
patterns.description: A rising power built artificial islands and militarized them to assert sweeping maritime claims via incremental faits accomplis below the threshold of war.
patterns.what_happened: China dredged reefs into bases, deployed coast guard and militia fleets, and asserted a 'nine-dash line' rejected by an international tribunal (2016). It ignored the ruling and entrenched control.
patterns.outcome: De facto Chinese dominance over contested waters grew despite legal defeat and freedom-of-navigation operations, demonstrating gray-zone salami tactics at sea.
patterns.lessons: Incremental, sub-threshold seizures ('salami slicing') accumulate into a fait accompli that's hard to reverse. Legal rulings without enforcement don't deter a determined revisionist; gray-zone tools blur the line for war.

patterns.name: Russian Full-Scale Invasion of Ukraine
patterns.description: A great power gambled on a quick decapitation of a neighbor, miscalculated resistance and unity, and triggered a long attritional war reshaping the European order.
patterns.what_happened: Expecting Kyiv to fall in days, Russia launched a multi-front invasion. Fierce Ukrainian resistance, Western arms and sweeping sanctions blunted the assault; the war settled into grinding attrition and energy/economic warfare.
patterns.outcome: Ukraine survived as a state, NATO expanded (Finland, Sweden) and unified, Russia absorbed huge costs and turned to China/Iran/North Korea — a wholesale reordering of European security.
patterns.lessons: Decapitation gambles rest on flawed assumptions about will and unity; they can backfire into the very encirclement they sought to prevent. Long wars are decided by industrial depth, alliances and resolve, not opening blitz.

patterns.name: Munich Agreement and Appeasement
patterns.description: Great powers conceded a vulnerable nation's territory to an aggressor in hope of buying peace, only to whet the appetite they meant to satisfy.
patterns.what_happened: To avoid war, Britain and France pressured Czechoslovakia to cede the Sudetenland to Hitler at Munich, without Czech consent. Hitler promised it was his last claim; months later he seized the rest of Czechoslovakia and then invaded Poland.
patterns.outcome: Appeasement failed utterly: it strengthened the aggressor, demoralized potential allies, and is the archetypal warning against feeding territorial demands.
patterns.lessons: Concessions to an expansionist aggressor typically buy time for the aggressor, not for peace. Sacrificing a small state's sovereignty to a predator signals weakness and invites the next demand.

patterns.name: Marshall Plan
patterns.description: A hegemon used large-scale economic reconstruction aid to bind allies, stabilize societies against a rival ideology, and build an enduring bloc.
patterns.what_happened: The US channeled massive aid to rebuild war-torn Western Europe, conditioned on cooperation and market integration. The USSR forbade its sphere from participating, deepening the continent's division.
patterns.outcome: Western Europe recovered and integrated under US leadership, communism's appeal in the West faded, and an Atlantic bloc consolidated — economic statecraft as alliance-building.
patterns.lessons: Reconstruction and prosperity are strategic weapons: they buy alignment more durably than arms. Generous, conditioned investment can out-compete a rival ideology for societies on the fence.

patterns.name: Cuban Embargo — Long Sanctions Regime
patterns.description: A decades-long comprehensive embargo aimed to topple a regime by economic strangulation, but instead entrenched it while imposing chronic hardship.
patterns.what_happened: After Cuba's revolution and nationalizations, the US imposed an ever-tightening embargo to force political change. The regime survived for over six decades, leaning on the embargo as a nationalist rallying point and external scapegoat.
patterns.outcome: The embargo neither toppled the government nor changed its system; it became a textbook case of long-run sanctions failing to achieve regime change while harming ordinary people.
patterns.lessons: Comprehensive sanctions can entrench rather than topple a regime by providing a unifying external enemy. Coercion without an achievable exit ramp can ossify into a self-perpetuating, ineffective policy.

patterns.name: Cuban Missile Crisis Quarantine — Naval Blockade as Signal
patterns.description: A carefully labeled 'quarantine' rather than 'blockade' let a power physically interdict cargo while leaving the adversary room to turn back without firing.
patterns.what_happened: Rather than airstrikes, the US chose a naval cordon around Cuba, calling it a 'quarantine' to avoid the casus-belli connotation of 'blockade,' and let Soviet ships visibly halt and reverse course.
patterns.outcome: The graduated, face-saving option gave Khrushchev an exit and bought negotiating time, becoming a model of calibrated coercion and signaling.
patterns.lessons: Word choice and graduated options create off-ramps in a crisis. A blockade framed to preserve the adversary's face can coerce without forcing a fight.

patterns.name: Mexican Drug War Militarization
patterns.description: A state's military crackdown on criminal networks fragmented them into more violent splinters, blurring the line between law enforcement and counterinsurgency.
patterns.what_happened: Mexico deployed the army against drug cartels with US support (Mérida Initiative). 'Kingpin' strategy decapitated leaders but splintered organizations into rival factions, driving record violence.
patterns.outcome: Tens of thousands killed and disappeared, cartels diversified and entrenched, and the militarized approach failed to restore security — a cautionary tale about decapitation in criminal-insurgent contexts.
patterns.lessons: Decapitating networked organizations can multiply violence by fracturing them into competing successors. Treating a socio-economic problem purely as a military one rarely produces durable security.

patterns.name: Helsinki Accords
patterns.description: A détente-era bargain recognized postwar borders in exchange for human-rights commitments that the recognizing power later wielded against the borders' guarantor.
patterns.what_happened: The Helsinki Final Act accepted Europe's borders (a Soviet aim) while enshrining human-rights principles (Basket III). Dissident 'Helsinki monitoring' groups then used those commitments to pressure communist regimes from within.
patterns.outcome: What looked like a Soviet win seeded internal opposition movements that helped erode the bloc's legitimacy over the following 15 years.
patterns.lessons: Concessions embedded in a deal can become long-fuse weapons for the other side. Normalization that locks in norms can empower domestic actors the signatory cannot control.

patterns.name: Operation Desert Shield to Storm — Coalition Assembly
patterns.description: A power converted a clear act of aggression into a broad, financed, UN-blessed coalition — including the aggressor's neighbors — through patient legitimacy-building.
patterns.what_happened: Before liberating Kuwait, Washington spent months assembling 35 nations, securing UN resolutions, basing rights and financial contributions (Gulf states and Japan/Germany funding much of the cost), and isolating Iraq diplomatically.
patterns.outcome: The coalition's breadth lent legitimacy and shared the burden, and the war's success (with limited aims) became a model — and a high bar — for multilateral intervention.
patterns.lessons: Legitimacy is manufactured through diplomacy, UN cover, and burden-sharing before the first shot. Broad coalitions constrain war aims but multiply legitimacy and staying power.

patterns.name: Arab Spring
patterns.description: A regional cascade of popular uprisings toppled several autocrats but produced divergent outcomes — democracy, civil war, restoration and crackdown — depending on militaries and outside intervention.
patterns.what_happened: Sparked in Tunisia, protests spread across the Arab world. Some leaders fell quickly (Tunisia, Egypt), others fought (Libya, Syria) drawing in foreign powers, and others crushed dissent with Gulf backing (Bahrain).
patterns.outcome: Only Tunisia briefly democratized; Egypt saw counter-revolution, Libya and Syria descended into war and proxy intervention, and the region's authoritarian resilience reasserted itself.
patterns.lessons: Revolutionary cascades share a spark but diverge based on the security forces' cohesion and outside intervention. Toppling a dictator is the easy part; what follows hinges on institutions and external meddling.

patterns.name: Nord Stream and Energy as Leverage
patterns.description: A supplier cultivated deep energy dependence as a coercive lever, while buyers discovered too late that interdependence cut both ways and could be weaponized.
patterns.what_happened: Russia expanded gas exports and pipelines (Nord Stream) to Europe, especially Germany, deepening dependence. Repeated gas cutoffs to transit states and the 2022 squeeze turned energy into an explicit weapon.
patterns.outcome: Europe scrambled to diversify off Russian gas at high cost after 2022; the episode discredited the assumption that commercial interdependence guarantees peace.
patterns.lessons: Dependence is a leash the supplier can pull; 'interdependence' is asymmetric and can be weaponized. Strategic vulnerabilities built for cheap commerce become coercion vectors in a crisis.

patterns.name: Indo-Pakistani Partition and Wars
patterns.description: A hasty colonial partition along religious lines produced mass displacement, a contested border region, and recurring wars between the successor states.
patterns.what_happened: British India was split into India and Pakistan in 1947 amid catastrophic communal violence and the largest mass migration in history. Kashmir's disputed status sparked wars in 1947–48 and 1965; 1971 birthed Bangladesh.
patterns.outcome: Two (later three) nuclear-armed-trending rivals, an unresolved Kashmir dispute, and a template for how rushed partition seeds permanent instability.
patterns.lessons: Drawing borders fast along identity lines guarantees displacement and durable grievance. Unresolved territorial disputes at independence become generational flashpoints.

patterns.name: Falklands War
patterns.description: A struggling regime seized disputed islands for a nationalist boost, misreading the distant claimant's resolve, and was militarily reversed at great political cost to itself.
patterns.what_happened: Argentina's junta invaded the British-held Falklands to rally domestic support amid economic crisis. Britain dispatched a naval task force across the Atlantic and retook the islands in a short, sharp war.
patterns.outcome: Britain prevailed and Thatcher's standing soared; the Argentine junta, discredited by defeat, fell — a vivid lesson on misjudging a faraway adversary's will.
patterns.lessons: Regimes start wars for domestic survival and routinely misjudge the other side's resolve over 'minor' territory. Defeat in a war of choice can topple the regime that launched it.

patterns.name: Israel–Hezbollah 2006 War
patterns.description: A conventional state army fought a well-armed non-state proxy to an ambiguous draw, revealing the resilience of networked, externally-supplied irregular forces.
patterns.what_happened: After a Hezbollah cross-border raid, Israel launched a major air and ground campaign in Lebanon. Hezbollah, armed by Iran and Syria, absorbed the blows, kept firing rockets, and survived intact.
patterns.outcome: An inconclusive ceasefire left Hezbollah politically strengthened for having 'withstood' Israel, illustrating how survival equals victory for a non-state actor.
patterns.lessons: Against a non-state proxy, not losing is winning; conventional superiority struggles to deliver decisive results. External arming and embedded social roots make such actors hard to defeat by force.

patterns.name: US Withdrawal from Afghanistan
patterns.description: After two decades of nation-building, a great power withdrew and watched the government and army it had built collapse in days, exposing the hollowness of imposed institutions.
patterns.what_happened: Following a deal with the Taliban, US and NATO forces withdrew. The Afghan government and security forces — long propped up by foreign money and airpower — disintegrated almost overnight as the Taliban swept to Kabul.
patterns.outcome: The Taliban retook power, a chaotic evacuation ensued, and the episode became a stark lesson in the limits of externally-built states and the dangers of mistaking dependence for capacity.
patterns.lessons: Institutions sustained by foreign money and force have no independent foundation and collapse when support ends. Withdrawal timelines reveal how little was actually built; sunk costs don't create legitimacy.
